skill.name: unity-ecs-patterns
skill.description: Master Unity ECS (Entity Component System) with DOTS, Jobs, and Burst for high-performance game development. Use when building data-oriented games, optimizing performance, or working with large entity counts.
skill.summary: Master Unity ECS patterns with DOTS for high-performance game development
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Security analysis confirms this is a legitimate educational resource for Unity ECS game development patterns. All static findings are false positives: markdown code fences were misidentified as shell commands, Unity API classes (SystemAPI, ECB) were flagged as system interactions, and math functions were incorrectly flagged as weak crypto. No actual security risks detected.

content.user_title: Build High-Performance Games with Unity ECS
content.value_statement: Learn production-ready Unity ECS patterns for building high-performance games with massive entity counts. This skill provides code examples for Entity Component System, Jobs, and Burst Compiler optimization.
actual_capabilities: Explain ECS architecture versus traditional OOP game patterns
Provide code examples for component, system, and entity creation
Show how to use Entity Command Buffers for structural changes
Demonstrate Job System and Burst Compiler optimization patterns
Explain baking and authoring workflows for ECS conversion
Provide singleton and query patterns for game state management
limitations: Does not include actual Unity project files or compiled binaries
Cannot execute Unity code or validate in Unity Editor
Covers patterns but assumes familiarity with C# and Unity basics
Does not replace official Unity DOTS documentation

use_cases.title: Learning ECS Fundamentals
use_cases.description: New developers learning Unity DOTS can understand core ECS concepts through clear code examples comparing OOP to data-oriented patterns.

use_cases.title: Optimizing Game Performance
use_cases.description: Experienced developers can apply Job System and Burst patterns to optimize CPU-bound game logic for massive entity counts.

use_cases.title: Converting OOP to ECS
use_cases.description: Teams migrating existing Unity games can use the baking and authoring patterns to convert GameObjects to entities.

prompt_templates.title: Basic ECS Question
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain the difference between ECS and traditional OOP in Unity. When should I use ECS over standard GameObjects?

prompt_templates.title: Create Entity System
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to create a movement system in Unity ECS that moves entities forward using the Job System and Burst Compiler.

prompt_templates.title: Entity Command Buffer
prompt_templates.prompt: How do I use Entity Command Buffers to spawn and destroy entities safely within a system? Give me a code example.

prompt_templates.title: Performance Optimization
prompt_templates.prompt: What are the best practices for optimizing ECS systems with Burst Compiler? Show me patterns for achieving maximum performance.

output_examples.input: Explain when to use ISystem versus SystemBase in Unity ECS
output_examples.output: Use ISystem (unmanaged) for new code as it provides better performance and full Burst compatibility. ISystem is recommended for all new development. SystemBase is legacy and should only be used when you need managed components or UnityEngine integration.

output_examples.input: How do I create a component in Unity ECS?
output_examples.output: Create a struct implementing IComponentData. Components should be pure data with no methods. Example: public struct Speed : IComponentData { public float Value; }

output_examples.input: What is the Entity Command Buffer used for?
output_examples.output: The Entity Command Buffer (ECB) records structural changes like create, destroy, add component, or remove component. These changes are applied at a sync point after the job completes, preventing race conditions in parallel jobs.

best_practices: Use ISystem over SystemBase for better performance and Burst compatibility
Always apply [BurstCompile] attribute to systems and jobs for maximum performance
Batch structural changes using Entity Command Buffers instead of direct entity modifications
anti_patterns: Do not use managed types (classes) in components - they break Burst compilation
Avoid structural changes (create/destroy) directly in jobs - use ECB instead
Do not iterate entities with foreach in hot paths - use IJobEntity for parallel processing

faq.question: What is Unity ECS?
faq.answer: Unity ECS (Entity Component System) is a data-oriented approach to game development where data (components) is separated from behavior (systems). It enables better memory layout and parallel processing for high-performance games.

faq.question: Do I need to know C# well to use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes, you should have solid C# knowledge. ECS uses advanced C# features like structs, interfaces, and attributes. Familiarity with Unity basics is also required.

faq.question: Can I use this skill without Unity installed?
faq.answer: This skill provides patterns and code examples. To test and run the code, you need Unity Editor installed with the Entities package (com.unity.entities).

faq.question: What is the Burst Compiler?
faq.answer: Burst is a LLVM-based compiler that compiles C# jobs to highly optimized native machine code. It provides significant performance improvements (often 10x+) over standard managed C#.

faq.question: When should I NOT use ECS?
faq.answer: Avoid ECS for simple games with few entities, when rapid prototyping is priority, or when your team lacks ECS expertise. Traditional GameObjects are better for these cases.

faq.question: How do I convert existing GameObjects to entities?
faq.answer: Use the authoring workflow: create a MonoBehaviour (Authoring component), then create a Baker that converts it to entity data. The subscene workflow handles the conversion automatically.
